content:
Hi I've been trying the miscdev and platform abstraction.

I wanted to know what the best way to use a DevRes<IoMem> in the miscdevice is?
I've got it working using container_of!, but I was wondering if there is a way to achieve that without unsafe.
Can you explain more closely what you are trying to do? Where are you using container_of?

I've got a PlatformDriver struct that contains the miscdev and a Arc<Devres<IoMem>>.
In the MiscDevice open function I use container_ofto get a clone of the DevRes.

I was hoping to have better way to access that.

Code:
https://github.com/onestacked/linux/blob/rust_ledpwm_driver/drivers/misc/ledpwm.rs

Not intended to go upstream, I was mostly trying out if a University exercise would be possible to do with the Rust abstractions.

I guess the miscdevice stuff is missing a feature you need ... it's tricky because container_of isn't really correct here. If the LedPwmDriver gets unplugged, the destructor could run in parallel with open, which could result in a UAF on mapping since the destructors of struct fields run in declaration order.
One way to fix it is to make MiscDeviceRegistration into a container type instead, so that you store the struct miscdevice and some custom Rust data inside the registration.

I've looked around the code a bit and don't really know how to implement that, since MiscDeviceRegistration is #[repr(transparent)].

It would be good to be able to do something like this in safe code and I think that most non-example drivers would that.

Christian Schrefl said:

I've looked around the code a bit and don't really know how to implement that, since MiscDeviceRegistration is #[repr(transparent)].

It would be good to be able to do something like this in safe code and I think that most non-example drivers would that.

We can store driver’s data in registration, I do same thing in my uio abstraction

The miscdevice struct doesn't have a private data field.

struct miscdevice  {
    int minor;
    const char *name;
    const struct file_operations *fops;
    struct list_head list;
    struct device *parent;
    struct device *this_device;
    const struct attribute_group **groups;
    const char *nodename;
    umode_t mode;
};
We can store it on the Rust side after the struct miscdevice

The MiscDeviceRegistration currently has a #[repr(transparent)] currently:

#[repr(transparent)]
#[pin_data(PinnedDrop)]
pub struct MiscDeviceRegistration<T> {
    #[pin]
    inner: Opaque<bindings::miscdevice>,
    _t: PhantomData<T>,
}

So storing additional data here is not currently possible, but I don't think that the #[repr(transparent)] is really necessary.

So it should be possible to do:

/// A registration of a miscdevice.
///
/// # Invariants
///
/// `inner` is a registered misc device.
#[pin_data(PinnedDrop)]
pub struct MiscDeviceRegistration<T, D: Sync> {
    #[pin]
    inner: Opaque<bindings::miscdevice>,
    data: D,
    _t: PhantomData<T>,
}

Not sure what bounds are requied for D, or if it should be a associated type of the MiscDevice trait.

I've implemented it here: https://github.com/onestacked/linux/commit/50532522415082ffa260d3fbbe136ccb77567701

Added RegistrationData as a assoc type of MiscDevice, requiring Send + Sync.
And storing it in MiscDeviceRegistration, allowing open() to take a (shared) reference to it.

It will need some changes to some safety comments and rework the rust_misc_device.rs sample,
but it seems to work.
Yep, that's pretty much what I was thinking.
I would let register take data: impl PinInit<T::RegistrationData> to allow for pin-initialization of the extra data.
Also, RegistrationData only needs to be Sync. Send isn't necessary.
Do you want to submit a patch for this?
I'm also happy to contribute with motivation for the commit message.
Yeah I can submit a patch, do you want me to add a sugested-by tag for you?
okay

@Alice Ryhl In case you want to look over the changes before I send them to the Mailing list:
https://github.com/onestacked/linux/commits/b4/rust_miscdevice_registrationdata/
The patches you say it depend on are already in Greg's driver-core-next tree, so you can just say you depend on that.
I've updated the message (and rebased on driver-core-next).
Don't you need to put data into a UnsafePinned or Opaque similar to irq::Registration::handler?
there is no Aliasing, because the data can only be accessed through shared references once it is given to MiscDeviceRegistration.
It's not really possible to give a mutable reference because open might be called from multiple threads at the same time.

Christian Schrefl said:

there is no Aliasing, because the data can only be accessed through shared references once it is given to MiscDeviceRegistration.

But the owner of MiscDeviceRegistration has the mutable reference to the whole struct, right?
Right. That's probably not allowed, even though it's impossible to get a mutable reference to get a mutable reference to the data itself.

I.e. there could exist two references at the same time:

  1. Pin<&mut MiscDeviceRegistration> <- the owner of the registration.
  2. &T::RegistrationData <- in open() function.

And some discussion between me and @Gary Guo seems to suggest that compilers could assume a &mut T::RegistrationData from a Pin<&mut MiscDeviceRegstration> if data is not !Unpin.

Yeah I probably need to put it into a UnsafeCell.

Probably best to do something like Aliased<T> that Alice suggested here:

pub struct Aliased<T> {
    value: UnsafeCell<T>,
    _pin: PhantomPinned,
}
I don't know if UnsafeCell even changes this situation, since UnsafeCell only changes the aliasing rules for &UnsafeCell and not &mut UnsafeCell.
No, but PhantomPinned makes it !Unpin, which does do what we want.
And !Unpin allows sharing like this?
yeah
ideally this should be UnsafePinned, after that gets stabilized
I've implemented it here.
I think we could also make Opaque<T> a type alias to Aliased<MaybeUninit<T>>.
Maybe, but I don't think there's any advantage in that.
It would reduce code duplication, but probably not really worth it.
I think the code would be easier to understand if they're just separate. We can explicitly document that Opaque is for wrapping C structs without complicating the explanation of Opaque with Aliased.

Checkpatch complains that a line is to long, but rustfmt formats it like that.
Should I just ignore it?

And it complains that the link line is to long, I don't think that I should wrap the Link?

Sorry, not that used to the kernel workflow.
Minor spelling fix in the commit message.
Usually there would not be an empty line between Link and the rest of the tags
Also, there usually isn't a # symbol in links

So just:

....
This was origianally proposed for the IRQ abstractions [0], but also useful for other cases where
Data may be aliased, but always valid.

Link: https://lore.kernel.org/rust-for-linux/CAH5fLgiOASgjoYKFz6kWwzLaH07DqP2ph+3YyCDh2+gYqGpABA@mail.gmail.com [0]
Suggested-by: Alice Ryhl <aliceryhl@google.com>
Signed-off-by: Christian Schrefl <chrisi.schrefl@gmail.com>

Yes.

But more generally, the commit message should explain why the change is being made, not just what it does.

And it complains that the link line is to long, I don't think that I should wrap the Link?

I just wrapped the text wrong sorry.

Here's the motivation I would give for the main change

rust: miscdevice: Add additional data to MiscDeviceRegistration

When using the Rust miscdevice bindings, you generally embed the
MiscDeviceRegistration within another struct:

struct MyDriverData {
    data: SomeOtherData,
    misc: MiscDeviceRegistration<MyMiscFile>
}

In the `fops->open` callback of the miscdevice, you are given a
reference to the registration, which allows you to access its fields.
For example, as of commit 284ae0be4dca ("rust: miscdevice: Provide
accessor to pull out miscdevice::this_device") you can access the
internal `struct device`. However, there is still no way to access the
`data` field in the above example, because you only have a reference to
the registration.

Using container_of is also not possible to do safely. For example, if
the destructor of `MyDriverData` runs, then the destructor of `data`
would run before the miscdevice is deregistered, so using container_of
to access `data` from `fops->open` could result in a UAF. A similar
problem can happen on initialization if `misc` is not the last field to
be initialized.

To provide a safe way to access user-defined data stored next to the
`struct miscdevice`, make `MiscDeviceRegistration` into a container that
can store a user-provided piece of data. This way, `fops->open` can
access that data via the registration, since the data is stored inside
the registration.

The container enforces that the additional user data is initialized
before the miscdevice is registered, and that the miscdevice is
deregistered before the user data is destroyed. This ensures that access
to the userdata is safe.

For the same reasons as in commit 88441d5c6d17 ("rust: miscdevice:
access the `struct miscdevice` from fops->open()"), you cannot access
the user data in any other fops callback than open. This is because a
miscdevice can be deregistered while there are still open files.

Thank you, for both the motivation and the implementation help!

Is it usual to use `` for marking types/code in the mailing list, I think I've seen it done both ways?

I've added a bit at the end talking about my issue in particular.

Do you want any acknowledgement other than the Suggested-by tag?

....
miscdevice can be deregistered while there are still open files.

A situation where this user data might be required is when a platform driver
acquires a resource in `probe` and wants to use this resource in the
`fops` implementation of a `MiscDevice`.

Suggested-by: Alice Ryhl <aliceryhl@google.com>
Signed-off-by: Christian Schrefl <chrisi.schrefl@gmail.com>
Using backticks in the commit message is a thing that some people do. You can choose to do it or not.
The Suggested-by tag is fine.
Do you want me to send it out or are you still looking it over?
I'm done looking for today, so go ahead and send. If I notice anything further, I can comment on the list.
